source_paper_title: Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework
source_paper_bibtex: @article{El_Balghiti_2023, title={Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework}, volume={48}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={El Balghiti, Othman and Elmachtoub, Adam N. and Grigas, Paul and Tewari, Ambuj}, year={2023}, month=nov, pages={2043–2065} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).

Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
title: Extend vector-contraction inequalities for Rademacher complexity to general norms
background_latex:
Let \(\mathcal X\) be an input (feature) space and \(\mathcal H\) be a class of vector-valued hypotheses \(f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\). Fix an i.i.d. sample \(x_1,\dots,x_n\in\mathcal X\). The (multivariate) empirical Rademacher complexity of \(\mathcal H\) is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\sigma}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma_i^\top f(x_i)\Big],
\]
where \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n\in\{\pm1\}^d\) have i.i.d. Rademacher coordinates and \(\mathbb E_\sigma\) denotes expectation over these signs.

Let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a norm on \(\mathbb R^d\). Consider a real-valued function \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) that is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), i.e.,
\[
|\phi(u)-\phi(v)|\le L\,\|u-v\|\quad\text{for all }u,v\in\mathbb R^d.
\]
Define the composed scalar class \(\phi\circ\mathcal H:=\{x\mapsto \phi(f(x)):\ f\in\mathcal H\}\). Its empirical Rademacher complexity is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\epsilon}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i\,\phi(f(x_i))\Big],
\]
where \(\epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{\pm1\}\) are i.i.d. Rademacher signs.

A vector-contraction inequality is a bound that controls \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\) in terms of \(L\) and \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\) (or a closely related complexity), possibly with a constant depending on the choice of norm \(\|\cdot\|\). Known general results (e.g., Maurer 2016) provide such inequalities in the case \(\|\cdot\|=\|\cdot\|_2\), and some related results exist for \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\), but general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms) are not covered by the currently available contraction tools referenced by the paper.
problem_definition_latex:
Develop a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity for Lipschitz maps under a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on \(\mathbb R^d\): find conditions and an explicit bound of the form
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)\,L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)
\]
(or an analogous bound in terms of a suitable multivariate Rademacher complexity), where \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), \(\mathcal H\subseteq\{f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\}\), and \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) is an explicit constant or function of the norm, extending the known \(\ell_2\)-based vector-contraction inequality to general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms).
latex:

\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)\,L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)

location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).
open_problem_quote:
Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a general inequality holding uniformly over function classes and Lipschitz maps, rather than for existence/nonexistence on a finite instance family. Automated search might find toy counterexamples for candidate bounds, but the relevant quantifiers and norm-dependent constants make systematic finite search unlikely to be decisive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could explore candidate inequalities by proposing norm-dependent constants and testing them empirically on randomized finite-dimensional hypothesis classes and Lipschitz functions to search for violations or plausible sharp constants. However, the ultimate goal is a universal proof (or a rigorous counterexample construction), so computational feedback would be only a heuristic guide.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper’s margin-based generalization bounds (Theorems 4–5) hinge on a vector-contraction step: bounding the scalar Rademacher complexity of the composed class \(\{x\mapsto \ell^{\gamma}_{\mathrm{SPO}}(f(x),c)\}\) by a Lipschitz constant times a multivariate Rademacher complexity \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)=\mathbb E\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac1n\sum_i \sigma_i^\top f(x_i)\). They explicitly restrict to the \(\ell_2\) setting because the most general available contraction lemma they know is Maurer’s 2016 inequality, which is Hilbertian in nature. The open problem asks for an extension to a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) (e.g., \(\ell_q\)), ideally yielding \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\) or a closely related complexity with an explicit constant.

As of the information provided here (no forward-citation analyses were supplied), there is no identified paper that resolves this extension in full generality. The natural mathematical pathway is through empirical process theory in Banach spaces: for a general norm, the right constant \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) typically depends on geometric parameters of the normed space (or its dual), such as type-2 constants, 2-smoothness/uniform smoothness, or related KhintchineKahane inequalities. Classical references by LedouxTalagrand and Pisier develop the machinery that would be used to derive such norm-dependent contraction/comparison results, and they suggest that for \(\ell_q\) norms one should expect constants scaling like \(\sqrt{q-1}\) (for \(q\ge2\)) or dimension-dependent behavior when the space lacks type 2.

Consequently, the problem remains open in the sense articulated by the source paper: a clean, general-purpose vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity under arbitrary norms, with explicit constants usable in learning-theoretic bounds like Theorem 4, is not currently pinned down by the provided citation set. Promising directions include proving contraction under assumptions such as uniform smoothness of the dual norm, or restricting to norm families (e.g., \(\ell_q\), group norms, Schatten norms) where sharp type/smoothness constants are known, thereby yielding explicit \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) suitable for predict-then-optimize generalization analyses.

sources.title: A vector-contraction inequality for Rademacher complexities
sources.bibtex:
@inproceedings{Maurer2016VectorContraction,
  author    = {Andreas Maurer},
  title     = {A vector-contraction inequality for Rademacher complexities},
  booktitle = {International Conference on Algorithmic Learning Theory (ALT)},
  year      = {2016},
  publisher = {Springer},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves a general vector-contraction inequality for Lipschitz maps �\(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) when Lipschitzness is measured in \(\ell_2\), bounding \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\) by a constant times \(L\) times a multivariate Rademacher complexity of \(\mathcal H\).
sources.connection: This is the specific contraction tool cited as [17] in the source paper and is the baseline that the open problem asks to extend from \(\ell_2\) to general norms.

sources.title: Probability in Banach Spaces: Isoperimetry and Processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops majorizing measure/generic chaining and comparison inequalities for Rademacher/Gaussian processes indexed by subsets of Banach spaces, including type/cotype and KhintchineKahane inequalities that control vector-valued Rademacher sums under general norms.
sources.connection: General-norm vector contraction is closely tied to Banach-space geometry (type 2, smoothness, martingale type). This monograph provides the foundational tools typically used to obtain norm-dependent constants \(C(\|\cdot\|)\).

sources.title: Factorization of linear operators and geometry of Banach spaces
sources.theorem_or_result: Relates bounds on Rademacher/Gaussian averages in Banach spaces to geometric parameters such as type/cotype and 2-smoothness, yielding dimension-dependent constants for \(\ell_q\) spaces (e.g., \(\sqrt{q-1}\) for \(q\ge2\)).
sources.connection: A plausible route to \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) for general norms is via type-2 or smoothness constants of the dual space; Pisier's results are standard references for such constants, especially for \(\ell_q\) norms.

sources.title: A new look at independence
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces sharp inequalities for suprema of empirical processes and decoupling/comparison techniques that often underlie contraction and symmetrization arguments beyond Hilbert spaces.
sources.connection: General-norm contraction may require refined comparison/decoupling beyond the Hilbertian case; Talagrand's techniques are frequently used in modern empirical process theory for Banach-valued classes.

sources.title: Understanding Machine Learning: From Theory to Algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Textbook treatment of Rademacher complexity, contraction for scalar Lipschitz maps, and multiclass/margin bounds; provides standard symmetrization and contraction templates.
sources.connection: While it does not solve the general-norm vector contraction question, it provides the standard scalar contraction lemma and proof patterns that one would try to generalize to vector-valued settings.
